title: Jerboa-specific security audit tool
description: A dedicated security scanner that understands Jerboa's security modules (taint, capability, restrict, sanitize, privsep) and can detect misuse patterns specific to Jerboa - like using sanitize-html in attribute context, missing taint checks at sinks, bare read without read-eval #f, and copy-environment without restrict.
use_case: When reviewing Jerboa code for security vulnerabilities, especially AI-generated code that may try to bypass the capability system.
example_scenario: User writes a web handler that uses sanitize-html for an href attribute. The tool detects this is the wrong sanitizer for the context and suggests sanitize-url-attribute.

title: Import policy validation in build pipeline
description: A build-time check that runs (std security import-audit) audit-imports-directory and fails the build if forbidden imports are found in user modules. Integrates with jerboa_build_and_report.
use_case: When building a Jerboa project, automatically detect AI-generated code that bypasses the capability system via direct (chezscheme) imports.
example_scenario: A PR adds a new module with (import (chezscheme)). The build fails with a clear message about the import policy violation, listing the exact file and line.

title: Rust static library musl build automation tool
description: A tool that automates building Rust static libraries for the musl target using the rustup-managed toolchain (not Homebrew/system Rust). Would detect the correct toolchain path, verify the x86_64-unknown-linux-musl target is installed, set CC=musl-gcc for the cc crate, and run cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl. Reports the output .a path and any missing dependencies.
use_case: When integrating a Rust crate as a static library into a Chez Scheme musl binary. Currently requires manually finding the right rustup toolchain, setting env vars, and verifying target installation.
example_scenario: User wants to add a new Rust crate (e.g., argon2 for password hashing) to their Chez Scheme project. The tool detects rustup's active toolchain, ensures musl target is installed, builds the crate, and reports the .a file location ready for linking.

title: Audit Sforeign_symbol registrations against linked .a symbols
description: A tool that cross-references Sforeign_symbol() registration calls in the static binary entry point (main.c or boot file) against actual symbols exported by linked .a/.o files (via nm). Detects missing registrations that cause 'foreign-procedure not found' errors at runtime, and dead registrations for symbols that no longer exist.
use_case: When adding or removing FFI functions from a Rust or C library linked into a static Chez binary. Missing Sforeign_symbol calls only fail at runtime when the foreign-procedure is first called, making them hard to catch.
example_scenario: User adds 5 new Rust FFI functions but only registers 4 of them with Sforeign_symbol. The tool reports the missing registration before the binary is even run, saving a debugging cycle.

title: Make safe APIs the default prelude exports
description: The (jerboa prelude) should re-export safe-sqlite-open as sqlite-open, safe-tcp-connect as tcp-connect, etc. Claude and humans will always reach for the simplest import — if the safe version IS the default, nobody has to remember to use (std safe). Raw APIs should require explicit (import (std db sqlite-native)) opt-in.
use_case: Every time Claude writes Jerboa code, it imports (jerboa prelude). If that gives it the safe APIs by default, all generated code is automatically type-checked and resource-safe without Claude knowing about the safety modules.
example_scenario: Claude writes (import (jerboa prelude)) then (sqlite-open "db") — this silently calls safe-sqlite-open which validates types, and (with-resource ...) is the documented pattern. No separate (import (std safe)) needed.

title: Lint rule warning on raw FFI module imports
description: A lint pass that detects (import (std db sqlite-native)) and warns 'consider (std safe) for type-checked wrappers'. Also warns on bare (error ...) calls suggesting structured conditions from (std error conditions), and on fork-thread suggesting (std concur structured).
use_case: Code review automation. Before merging, lint catches unsafe patterns that Claude or humans introduced. Much cheaper than debugging runtime crashes.
example_scenario: PR adds a module with (import (std net tcp-raw)). Lint says: 'WARNING: raw TCP import — use (std safe) safe-tcp-connect for type validation and port range checking'.

title: Generate safe-by-default prelude module from unsafe module exports
description: A tool that takes a list of unsafe modules and their safe wrapper module, then generates a prelude .ss file that re-exports the safe wrappers under the original (unsafe) names. Handles prefix stripping (safe-sqlite-open → sqlite-open), import/export generation, and excludes specified dangerous exports (c-lambda, foreign-procedure). Would automate the manual process of creating (jerboa prelude safe) style modules.
use_case: When a project has raw FFI modules and safe wrapper modules, and wants to create a convenience prelude that shadows unsafe names with safe equivalents — common pattern for security-hardened Scheme libraries.
example_scenario: jerboa has (std db sqlite-native) with sqlite-open and (std safe) with safe-sqlite-open. Creating (jerboa prelude safe) required manually listing ~40 exports, writing define aliases for each (define sqlite-open safe:safe-sqlite-open), and carefully excluding raw FFI forms. A generator tool could produce this from a mapping spec in one call.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per prelude module — eliminates manual export listing, alias writing, and import construction

title: Generate self-signed mTLS cert+key for testing
description: A tool that generates a self-signed Ed25519 certificate and private key suitable for mTLS testing. Calls the existing (std crypto x509) cert generation, then validates the cert is usable as both server cert and client CA (self-signed mTLS pattern). Returns file paths ready to pass to rustls-server-ctx-new-mtls and rustls-connect-mtls.
use_case: When setting up mTLS for jerboa-shell or testing TLS code. Currently requires manually generating certs with openssl or the x509 module, then knowing that the same cert works for server, client, and CA in the self-signed case.
example_scenario: User wants to test mTLS server/client. Tool generates cert.pem + key.pem, validates they load in rustls, and prints the exact rustls-server-ctx-new-mtls and rustls-connect-mtls calls to use.

title: Cross-platform sandbox feature parity checker
description: A tool that compares sandbox capabilities across Linux (seccomp/Landlock), FreeBSD (Capsicum), and macOS (Seatbelt). Reports which features are available, which have parity, and which are platform-specific. Would read the actual (std security sandbox), (std security capsicum), (std security seccomp), and (std security seatbelt) modules to determine real coverage.
use_case: When adding new sandbox features, to verify parity across platforms. Currently requires manually reading each platform module and comparing exports/presets.
example_scenario: User adds a new seccomp preset on Linux. Tool shows that FreeBSD/Capsicum has a matching preset but macOS/Seatbelt does not, prompting the user to add the Seatbelt equivalent.

title: compile_check should test module in isolation, not via prelude
description: jerboa_compile_check currently seems to transitively import the prelude when checking a library. If the prelude itself has errors (e.g., invalid except clause), compile_check fails on unrelated modules like (std misc list) with the prelude's error. It should compile the target library in isolation, only loading its declared imports — not the full prelude chain. This would make compile_check reliable even when the prelude is broken.
use_case: When iterating on library code while the prelude has a known issue. compile_check should still work on libraries that don't depend on the prelude.
example_scenario: The prelude has an invalid (except (std sugar) ... while ...) clause. Running jerboa_compile_check on lib/std/misc/list.sls (which imports only (chezscheme)) fails with the prelude error instead of checking list.sls. Had to fall back to running scheme directly via bash to verify the module compiled.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per failed compile-check that forces bash fallback

title: compile_check should use Chez reader for .sls files with #!chezscheme
description: jerboa_compile_check uses the Jerboa reader (which converts [...] to (list ...)) even for .sls library files that start with #!chezscheme. This causes false compilation failures on any library using brackets in syntax-case patterns — including the existing (std typed) module. The tool should detect #!chezscheme at the top of a file and use the standard Chez reader instead of the Jerboa reader, matching what `scheme --libdirs` does at build time.
use_case: When writing or modifying any .sls library that uses brackets in syntax-case, define-syntax, or let bindings — which is nearly all of them.
example_scenario: Writing lib/std/ergo.sls with a define-syntax using syntax-case and [(_ expr type) ...] patterns. jerboa_compile_check returns 'invalid syntax-case clause (list (_ expr type) ...)' because [...] was read as (list ...). Had to skip compile_check entirely and rely on make build, losing the fast feedback loop.
estimated_token_reduction: ~200 tokens per compile-check call that fails spuriously, plus eliminates need to run bash make as workaround. Affects nearly every .sls file edit session.

title: IPC REPL: trace command dispatch path for a given command name
description: A tool or REPL helper that traces which branch of cmd-* dispatch fires for a given command in the current editor state — showing which buffer-type predicate matched, what guard conditions passed/failed, and which leaf function was called. Currently diagnosing "why does backspace do nothing?" requires manually querying (terminal-buffer? buf), (shell-buffer? buf), (hash-get *shell-state* buf) etc. one at a time via nc to the IPC REPL.
use_case: When an editing command silently does nothing in a specific buffer type, trace which cond branch fired (or failed to fire) without manually querying each predicate.
example_scenario: User reports backspace doesn't work in shell script files. Root cause: shell-buffer? returns #t (lexer-lang=shell), but no shell-state entry exists, so all cond branches in cmd-backward-delete-char fail silently. Diagnosing this required 5+ separate nc REPL queries to check each predicate and state variable one at a time.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per debug session (eliminates 4-6 sequential REPL probe calls)

title: Audit boot file library list against transitive imports
description: A tool that parses a Chez static build script's boot file library list and compares it against the transitive import closure of the entry point. Detects libraries that are imported but missing from the boot file (causing runtime 'library not found' errors in static builds) and libraries listed in the boot file but never transitively imported (dead weight). Works by reading the build script's library list and walking the import graph via jerboa_module_deps.
use_case: When adding a new library dependency to a static Chez binary. Missing a single transitive dependency from the boot file causes a runtime error that only manifests when the code path is hit, making it hard to catch before deployment.
example_scenario: Added (jerboa-aws pssm) to the boot file but forgot its transitive dependency (jerboa-coreutils common security). The binary built fine but crashed at runtime when pssm tried to import security. Had to debug by trial and error, adding missing libraries one at a time. This tool would have reported all missing transitive deps in one pass.
estimated_token_reduction: ~1000 tokens per missing-library debugging cycle — eliminates 3-5 rounds of build/run/crash/add-library

title: jerboa_check_balance should track nesting depth accurately for complex files
description: jerboa_check_balance reports heuristic mismatches that don't correspond to actual errors. In this session, it reported 'Mismatched ] at line X — expected ) to close ( at line Y' for code that was actually off by one paren in a different place. The error messages point to wrong locations, making it hard to fix. A more accurate tracker that counts nesting depth per-form and reports 'expected N closing delimiters but found M' with the exact open positions would be more actionable. The tool should also handle mixed [/( usage (common in Chez cond clauses) without false positives.
use_case: When editing large Scheme files with deeply nested cond clauses using mixed [ and ( delimiters, and the balance is off by 1. The current tool reports misleading locations.
example_scenario: Added a 100-line cond clause to jsh.ss with 12 levels of nesting. Was off by one paren. jerboa_check_balance said 'Mismatched ] at line 1607 — expected ) to close ( at line 1520' but the actual fix was adding one ) before the ] at line 1607. Had to manually trace all opens/closes to find the issue, defeating the purpose of the tool.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per balance-fixing cycle — eliminates manual paren counting

title: Post-build static binary launch verification tool
description: A tool that runs a static binary with --version (or a configurable flag) after make static-qt and verifies it exits cleanly without exceptions. Currently, `make static-qt` succeeds but the binary can still crash at launch due to missing libraries, unresolved symbols, or stale compiled artifacts. The tool would run the binary, capture stderr, and report any Chez exceptions or segfaults — catching issues that only manifest at runtime in the static build.
use_case: After every make static-qt, automatically verify the binary launches without exceptions. Currently this is a manual step that Claude must remember to do (and has forgotten in the past, leading to broken deliverables).
example_scenario: Claude fixes a bug in highlight.ss, runs make build && make static-qt (both succeed), but the static binary crashes with 'attempt to reference unbound identifier restore-margin-colors!' because a cross-module reference wasn't available in the WPO build. Without the verification step, Claude would have reported the fix as done. The tool catches this before the user even tries.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per build cycle — eliminates the need to manually remember and run the verification step, and prevents wasted debugging cycles from broken binaries

title: Diagnose face registry binding failures in static Chez binaries
description: A diagnostic tool that checks if face-get returns #f for standard font-lock faces (font-lock-keyword-face, font-lock-string-face, etc.) and reports the face registry state. In WPO static builds, module-level bindings can fail silently — face-get returns #f even though load-theme! was called. The tool would dump *faces* hash table contents, verify theme loading, and compare against the expected theme definition. Currently diagnosing this required manual REPL queries that couldn't access module-private variables.
use_case: When tree-sitter or Scintilla highlighting shows all-gray text in the static binary but works in interpreted mode. The face registry is populated by load-theme! but face-get returns #f for all faces.
example_scenario: Tree-sitter highlighting was activated but all 19 style colors were gray (#d8d8d8). face-fg-rgb fell back to default for every face. Spent 30+ minutes diagnosing before discovering face-get returned #f in the static binary. Workaround: read colors directly from theme alist via theme-get instead of face-get.
estimated_token_reduction: ~500 tokens per face debugging session — eliminates multiple rounds of REPL probing

title: Validate patch-file! ordering dependencies in build scripts
description: A tool that analyzes a build script containing sequential patch-file! calls and detects ordering bugs — where a patch's match string depends on text introduced by a later patch. Parses each patch-file! call, simulates the transformation sequence, and reports patches whose old_string doesn't exist at their position in the sequence. Would also detect duplicate match strings and patches that silently fail (no match found).
use_case: When adding new post-build patches to build-jerboa.ss or similar build scripts with 20+ sequential patch-file! calls targeting the same file.
example_scenario: Added patch 23 to insert a git-branch-name import into main.sls, matching '(jsh recorder)\n   (jsh mux-server)'. But mux-server import is added by patch 30 in the Multiplexer section. The build completed without error but the import was silently missing, causing a runtime crash. Took multiple debug cycles to discover the ordering bug.
estimated_token_reduction: ~500 tokens per patch ordering bug — eliminates build/crash/debug cycle that typically takes 2-3 rounds

title: Diff prelude exports against stdlib modules
description: A tool that compares what (jerboa prelude) exports vs what each imported stdlib module exports, showing symbols that are imported but not re-exported. Helps ensure new features added to stdlib modules are surfaced in the prelude. Currently requires manually reading both prelude.sls and each module, comparing export lists — error-prone and token-heavy.
use_case: When adding new exports to stdlib modules (like std/misc/list or std/result), to verify they're also re-exported from (jerboa prelude). Prevents the recurring problem of features being available only via direct module import.
example_scenario: Added string-match?, string-find, string-find-all to (std misc string) but forgot to add them to (jerboa prelude). Users writing .ss files with (import (jerboa prelude)) couldn't access the new functions. A diff tool would have caught this immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~400 tokens per prelude sync — eliminates manual cross-referencing of export lists across 15+ modules

title: Visual regression testing via screenshot comparison
description: A tool that captures two screenshots of the running jemacs-qt (via the automation bridge) and computes a pixel-level diff. Would highlight changed regions, report percentage difference, and optionally save a diff image. Currently Claude can take screenshots and view them, but has no way to programmatically detect visual artifacts (gaps, wrong colors, misaligned panes) without manual inspection.
use_case: After fixing a flicker or rendering bug, verify that the fix didn't introduce new visual artifacts by comparing screenshots before and after an operation (e.g. split window, switch buffer, resize).
example_scenario: Testing vterm alt-screen rendering: take screenshot of top output, wait 2 seconds for refresh, take another screenshot. Diff should show only the process list changing, not gaps or blank rows appearing. Currently requires manual viewing of each screenshot.
estimated_token_reduction: ~200 tokens per visual test — eliminates manual screenshot inspection and narrows focus to changed regions

title: Batch command scaffolding tool for editor projects
description: A tool that takes a list of command names and patterns (echo-message, toggle-mode, echo-read-string) and generates the complete scaffolded function definitions, register-command! lines, and markdown doc table rows in one call. Currently scaffolding 20 commands requires ~6 sequential tool calls (read file ends, edit modes.ss, edit final.ss, edit regs2.ss, edit docs, build) with substantial boilerplate repetition.
use_case: When adding many scaffolded editor commands in bulk rounds — a common workflow for jerboa-emacs feature parity with GNU Emacs.
example_scenario: User asks to add 20 new Emacs commands per round. Each round requires reading 3 file endings, writing 10 function defs to modes.ss, 10 to final.ss, 20 register-command! lines to regs2.ss, and a 20-row markdown table to docs. With a batch tool, one call with [{name: "foo-mode", type: "toggle"}, {name: "foo-bar", type: "echo", msg: "Foo: did bar"}] generates all artifacts.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000 tokens per round (eliminates 4 large edit blocks of repetitive boilerplate)

title: Verify binary hardening properties (PIE, RELRO, stack canaries, CET)
description: A tool that reads an ELF binary and reports its security hardening status: PIE (position-independent executable), Full RELRO (BIND_NOW), stack canaries (__stack_chk_fail references), NX stack, CET (shadow stack / IBT via PT_GNU_PROPERTY), and FORTIFY_SOURCE. Returns a structured report with pass/fail per property and remediation advice for missing ones.
use_case: After building a hardened Chez Scheme or Jerboa static binary, verify all expected hardening properties are present. Currently requires manual readelf/nm invocations to check PIE (file command), RELRO (readelf -d | grep BIND_NOW), and stack canaries (nm | grep stack_chk).
example_scenario: Building Chez with --enable-harden. After the build, need to verify that libkernel.a objects have stack canaries, the final binary is PIE, and RELRO is enabled. Required three separate bash commands (file, readelf -d, nm on individual .o files) — a single tool call returning a hardening report would replace all of these.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per build verification — replaces 3-4 bash commands and result parsing

title: Detect prelude-shadowed Chez builtins causing unexpected errors
description: When evaluating Jerboa code that uses Chez builtins like `make-time`, `sort`, `printf`, etc., detect when the prelude has shadowed the builtin with an incompatible version and the call fails with a confusing type/arity error. Should report "Note: (jerboa prelude) shadows `make-time` — use `(let () (import (only (chezscheme) make-time)) make-time)` to capture the original." Currently Claude must know the full shadow list from CLAUDE.md and recognize the pattern manually.
use_case: When a standalone Jerboa prelude script tries to use `sleep` with `make-time 'time-duration`, it gets a cryptic error because the prelude's `make-time` expects datetime args. The detection would explain the shadow and point to the capture pattern.
example_scenario: Script has `(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 1))` — fails with wrong-type argument. The error message mentions `make-time` but gives no hint that the prelude shadowed it. Claude spent 2 tool-call round trips discovering the shadow and the fix pattern.
estimated_token_reduction: ~400 tokens per occurrence — eliminates 2-3 jerboa_eval probe calls to discover the shadow

title: Detect Qt parent-child double-free: explicit destroy after parent destroy
description: In Qt, destroying a QObject parent automatically destroys all child widgets. If code explicitly destroys a child widget after destroying its parent, this causes a double-free SIGSEGV (manifests as `QBindingStorage::clear()` crash in Qt 6). The tool should detect patterns in `vendor/qt_shim.cpp` or Scheme FFI call sequences where a child widget pointer is passed to `qt_widget_destroy` after its parent has already been destroyed in the same scope/path.
use_case: When refactoring Qt window management code that destroys splitter hierarchies. Qt's implicit child cleanup is easy to miss — the bug is silent until the stress test hits the specific code path.
example_scenario: In `qt-frame-delete-window!`, destroying `parent-spl` (a QSplitter) automatically destroys `container` (its child QWidget). Then the code unconditionally called `qt-widget-destroy! container` — double-free, SIGSEGV in `QBindingStorage::clear()`. Fix: set `container = #f` before destroying parent so the conditional destroy is skipped.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per crash investigation — Qt double-free crashes are very hard to trace without the pattern; eliminates multiple stress-burn-static runs and backtrace analysis

title: Compare two implementations of the same feature across repos
description: A tool that takes two file paths (or repo paths + feature names) and produces a structured diff of their capabilities — exported functions, supported features, known limitations, and architectural differences. Could work by running jerboa_file_summary and jerboa_module_exports on both sides and presenting a comparison table.
use_case: When evaluating whether to adopt a new library or replace an existing one — e.g. comparing two terminal emulator backends to decide what the current implementation is missing.
example_scenario: User asks: "what does ~/mine/emacs-libgterm offer over our current vterm?" Required: exploring both repos with separate Explore agents (two parallel subagent spawns, ~3000 tokens combined), then manually synthesizing the comparison. A dedicated tool taking two paths and a topic would return a structured table in one call.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000 tokens per comparison — replaces two parallel Explore agent spawns and manual synthesis

title: jerboa_prompt_to_code: Generate verified Jerboa from natural language prompts
description: Tool that takes pseudocode/prompts, generates Jerboa via LLM, then verifies with eval/syntax_check/verify loop, iterating until passing. Outputs working .ss snippet.
use_case: AI assistants generating safe, executable Scheme from user descriptions without manual fixes.
example_scenario: User: "Write a function to sort a list and compute sum." Tool: Generates def/sort/sum code, verifies, returns verified snippet.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per code gen session (auto-verify reduces back-and-forth).

title: jerboa_ai_scaffold: Generate ML/AI patterns from Rust crates
description: Scaffold Jerboa FFI wrappers for Rust ML libs (e.g., tch-rs tensors, ndarray stats); auto-generates safe bindings with type checks and examples.
use_case: Prototyping AI models in Jerboa without manual FFI boilerplate.
example_scenario: User: "Scaffold tensor ops from tch-rs." Tool: Generates foreign-procedure defs, safe wrappers, test suite.
estimated_token_reduction: ~1200 tokens (avoids explaining/generating FFI from scratch).

title: Enhance parallel tool execution for multi-step AI reasoning
description: Allow batching 5+ independent tools in one call (e.g., lint + verify + security_scan); return structured results for faster AI workflows.
use_case: AI analyzing codebases: Run full audit (syntax, deps, security) in one round-trip.
example_scenario: Batch: verify + dead_code + import_policy_check on project; AI gets all results at once.
estimated_token_reduction: ~400 tokens (fewer sequential calls).

title: jerboa_semantic_search: Embeddings-based search over stdlib and cookbooks
description: Vector search for functions/patterns by intent (e.g., "safe file read"); uses simple embeddings on docstrings/exports for code discovery.
use_case: AI finding relevant APIs without exact name knowledge.
example_scenario: Query: "Handle JSON errors safely." Returns: json-parse with try/catch examples from cookbook.
estimated_token_reduction: ~600 tokens (reduces grep/howto trial-and-error).
